coredata.dc:title: Oxygen vacancy engineering of TiO2 nanosheets for enhanced photothermal therapy against cervical cancer in the second near-infrared window
coredata.dc:creator: Bian, Lihua
Wang, Nan
Tuersong, Kelibiguli
Kaidierdan, Ayiguzaili
Li, Jinfeng
Gong, Jing
coredata.dc:description: Cervical cancer is the most common and deadly female cancer on the worldwide scale. Considering that the conventional surgery treatment and chemotherapy would cause certain side effects, photothermal therapy (PTT) possesses desired therapeutic efficiency and insignificant side effects against cervical cancer. However, the lack of efficient and safe photothermal agents that operate in the second near-infrared (NIR-II) window is a main obstacle hindering the clinical transformation of PTT. Titanium dioxide (TiO2)-based nanomaterials are commonly applied in the biomedicine field, but the weak absorption and low photothermal conversion efficiency (PCE) of TiO2 in the NIR region limit their applications in PTT. Herein, we report the oxygen vacancy engineering that is a robust strategy to regulate the electronic structures of TiO2 for photothermal conversion properties optimizing. The obtained oxygen vacancy-doped TiO2−x nanosheets exhibit strong NIR-II absorption and high PCE owing to their decreased bandgap. Specifically, the PCE of TiO2−x nanosheets is determined to be 69.5 % in the efficient NIR-II window, which is much higher than that of widely reported PTT agents. Complete tumor recession without recurrence or pulmonary metastasis is realized by enhanced NIR-II PTT via TiO2−x nanosheets at an ultralow and safe laser exposure (0.6 W/cm2). Our findings suggest that oxygen vacancy engineering of nanomaterials could regulate their photothermal conversion performances, promoting the further application of TiO2-based nanomaterials in the biomedical.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Oxygen vacancy
TiO2 nanosheets
Photothermal therapy
Second near-infrared window
Cervical cancer

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation and characterization of TiO2−x nanosheets
Enhanced photothermal properties of TiO2−x nanosheets
Deep-tissue photothermal properties of TiO2−x nanosheets
In vitro biocompatibility and NIR-II PTT

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Overall scheme to show the synthesis procedures of black TiO2−x nanosheets. (b, c) TEM and HRTEM images of TiO2−x nanosheets. (d-f) Hydrodynamic diameter (d), absorption spectra (e), and XRD patterns (f) of TiO2 and TiO2−x nanosheets. (g-i) Survey XPS spectrum (g), high-resolution Ti 2p (h), and O 1 s spectra (i) of TiO2−x nanosheets.

(a) Enhanced temperature variations of TiO2−x nanosheets under 1064 nm laser irradiation compared with TiO2 nanosheets. (b, c) Temperature variations curves of TiO2−x nanosheets with varied concentrations (0–0.3 mg/kg) under 1064 nm laser irradiation with varied power density (0.4–0.8 W/cm2). (d, e) The heating and cooling curves of TiO2 nanosheets under the treatment of 1064 nm laser (0.6 W/cm2) and the measurements of PCE. (f) Temperature variations curves of TiO2−x nanosheets under 1064 nm laser irradiation (0.6 W/cm2) after ten on/off cycles.

(a) Schematic illustration of the in vitro deep-tissue photothermal properties of TiO2−x nanosheets. (b) Temperature variations curves of TiO2−x nanosheets under the treatment of 808 or 1064 nm laser in the presence of additional tissue with varied thicknesses (0–10 mm).

(a, b) Relative cell viability of Hela cells treated with TiO2−x without (a) or with (b) the treatment of 1064 nm laser. (c) Live/dead cell staining of Hela cells after different treatments including control, 1064, TiO2−x, and TiO2−x + 1064.

(a) Schematic illustration of NIR-II PTT via TiO2−x nanosheets. (b, c) Time-dependent in vivo (b) and ex vivo (c) NIR imaging of mice after intravenous injection of ICG@TiO2−x. (d) The quantitative result of NIR imaging presented in (d). (e) ICP-MS measurements indicated the biodistribution of Ti element in major organs and tumors at 24 h-post-injection. (f-h) Tumor growth curves, survival curves, and H&E staining of the mice or tumors after different treatments.

head.ce:title: Oxygen vacancy engineering of TiO2 nanosheets for enhanced photothermal therapy against cervical cancer in the second near-infrared window

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Obstetrics and Gynecology, Beijing Anzhen Hospital, Capital Medical University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Obstetrics and Gynecology, Beijing Anzhen Hospital, Capital Medical University, Beijing, China.

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Cervical cancer is the most common and deadly female cancer on the worldwide scale. Considering that the conventional surgery treatment and chemotherapy would cause certain side effects, photothermal therapy (PTT) possesses desired therapeutic efficiency and insignificant side effects against cervical cancer. However, the lack of efficient and safe photothermal agents that operate in the second near-infrared (NIR-II) window is a main obstacle hindering the clinical transformation of PTT. Titanium dioxide (TiO2)-based nanomaterials are commonly applied in the biomedicine field, but the weak absorption and low photothermal conversion efficiency (PCE) of TiO2 in the NIR region limit their applications in PTT. Herein, we report the oxygen vacancy engineering that is a robust strategy to regulate the electronic structures of TiO2 for photothermal conversion properties optimizing. The obtained oxygen vacancy-doped TiO2−x nanosheets exhibit strong NIR-II absorption and high PCE owing to their decreased bandgap. Specifically, the PCE of TiO2−x nanosheets is determined to be 69.5 % in the efficient NIR-II window, which is much higher than that of widely reported PTT agents. Complete tumor recession without recurrence or pulmonary metastasis is realized by enhanced NIR-II PTT via TiO2−x nanosheets at an ultralow and safe laser exposure (0.6 W/cm2). Our findings suggest that oxygen vacancy engineering of nanomaterials could regulate their photothermal conversion performances, promoting the further application of TiO2-based nanomaterials in the biomedical.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Currently, cervical cancer remains the highest incidence diagnosed of malignant tumor in females. Photothermal therapy (PTT) using a light-absorbing agents to convert near-infrared (NIR) laser energy into local hyperthermia is considered to be a promising method to replace conventional approaches for treating cervical cancer [1–3]. The employment of NIR light as an external and remote manipulation brings distinct advantages such as simple procedures, fast recovery after cancer therapeutics, and minimized side effects [4,5]. To date, tremendous endeavors have been focused primarily on developing novel photothermal agents (e.g., organic compounds nanoparticles [6,7], carbon-based nanomaterials [8,9], and metal nanostructures [10]) in the first near-infrared (NIR-I, 700–950 nm) window. However, the photothermal agents in the second NIR window (NIR-II, 1000 −1350 nm), particularly within 1000–1100 nm, are more practical for clinical applications, because NIR-II window is recognized to possess deeper penetration depth, lower tissue self-heating, and higher maximum permissible exposure (MPE) compared to the widely studied NIR-I window [11–14]. Therefore, it is highly desired to explore efficient photothermal agents in the NIR-II window, particularly to treat tumors buried deep in tissue.

Recently, several groups pioneered the development of photothermal agents in NIR-II window, including transition metal sulfide/oxide semiconductors [15,16], noble metal nanomaterials [17], and polymer nanocomposites [18,19]. Despite significant progress, there are still some issues need to be addressed. For instance, the potential long-term toxicity and nonbiodegradable nature of heavy metals with high costs severely impede their clinical application [20,21]. Furthermore, the high irradiation doses of current NIR-II agents might hinder their applications in the PTT of cancer. In this regard, the exploration of PTT agents with low cost, good photostability, and strong absorption in the NIR-II window, which could generate enough heat irradiated by laser with low power density, thus becomes extremely important.

TiO2-based nanostructures have been widely explored for many applications, such as energy storage [22], environmental protection [23,24], photocatalytic [25,26], and biomedicine [27–29]. The stable structure, good biocompatibility, low toxicity, and unique electronic structure of TiO2 nanomaterials enabled them to be applied in tumor therapy, such as photodynamic therapy, sonodynamic therapy, drug delivery, and even in PTT [30,31]. However, the absorption of TiO2-based nanomaterials commonly limited in the UV and visible region, which possessed insufficient light penetration depth in tissues. With respect to PTT application, the TiO2 should possess the capability of NIR-responsive absorption to achieve the best tissue penetration without damaging the surrounding living tissues. Thus, developing the reasonable NIR-II-triggered TiO2 nanomaterials are of great significance for the applications in biomedicine.

In this work, we reported the oxygen vacancy engineering of white TiO2 nanosheets to obtain the black TiO2−x nanosheets with strong NIR-II absorption and conversion for performing efficient PTT against cervical cancer. Compared with TiO2 nanosheets, the oxygen-deficient TiO2−x nanosheets were promising photothermal agents owing to the decreased bandgap, leading to the obvious enhancements of NIR absorption and photothermal conversion efficiency (PCE). Under 808 or 1064 nm laser irradiation, the PCE of TiO2−x nanosheets were calculated to be 66.7 % and 69.5 %, respectively, which was much higher than that of widely reported PTT agents. In the meantime, the deep-tissue photothermal properties of TiO2−x nanosheets were also investigated in the efficient NIR-II window, which increased the temperature of TiO2−x solution to about 52 oC in the presence of additional tissue with the thickness of 10 mm. Through the efficient NIR-II PTT, complete tumor eradication of cervical cancer was achieved by TiO2−x nanosheets at the safe power density of 0.6 W/cm2. Our findings indicate that TiO2−x nanosheets with abundant oxygen vacancies are promising for further biomedicine applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation and characterization of TiO2−x nanosheets
ce:section.ce:para: The main synthesis procedures of black TiO2−x nanosheets with abundant oxygen vacancies were presented in Fig. 1a. Typically, we utilized a facile hydrothermal method to prepare the single-layer TiO2 nanosheets using tetrabutyl titanate and HF as the precursors. Then, NaBH4 was used as a reducing agent to synthesize the black TiO2−x nanosheets through a solid-phase reduction reaction. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) was then performed to investigate the morphology of TiO2−x nanosheets. As presented in Fig. 1b, TiO2−x nanosheets were flake-like and had a uniform shape with an average diameter of about 50 nm. The size was also determined by dynamic light scattering measurements, which revealed that the hydrodynamic diameter of TiO2−x nanosheets was 73.5 nm (Fig. 1d). Furthermore, the successful synthesis of TiO2−x nanosheets was confirmed by the high-resolution TEM image (Fig. 1c), showing a 0.35 nm crystal spacing, which was consistent with the (101) plane of anatase-phase TiO2. Interestingly, the absorption spectrum of TiO2−x nanosheets was extended to the NIR-II region (Fig. 1e), while that of TiO2 nanosheets was limited in the UV and visible light region. According to the absorption spectra, the bandgap of TiO2 and TiO2−x nanosheets was calculated to be 3.2 and 2.2 eV, respectively (Fig. S1), indicating that the introduction of oxygen vacancy could decrease the bandgap of TiO2 nanosheets and then shift their absorption to NIR-II region. We also investigated the stability of TiO2−x nanosheets in different media. As shown in Fig. S2, the clear TiO2−x aqueous, PBS, and DMEM solution without obvious precipitation was observed, suggesting their excellent colloidal stability in different media after storing for 3 days.

The chemical composition and structure characteristics of TiO2−x nanosheets were then investigated by X-ray diffraction (XRD), X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), and Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR). Fig. 1f showed the XRD patterns of TiO2 and TiO2−x nanosheets, revealing the (101), (004), (200), (211), and (204) plane, which was corresponded to the anatase-phase TiO2 nanostructure (JCPDS card no. 21–1272). The XRD pattern of TiO2−x nanosheets was similar to that of TiO2 nanosheets, which could be ascribed to the unchanged crystal structure of TiO2−x nanosheets after the doping of oxygen vacancy. The presence of Ti and O elements in TiO2 and TiO2−x nanosheets were demonstrated by their survey XPS spectra (Fig. 1g and S3a). Importantly, Ti3+ in the high-resolution Ti 2p spectrum and oxygen vacancy in the high-resolution O 1 s spectrum can be observed in TiO2−x nanosheets (Fig. 1h), while only Ti4+ and Ti-O were detected in those of TiO2 nanosheets (Fig. S3b), suggesting the successfully formation of oxygen vacancy in TiO2−x nanosheets. Furthermore, the presence of Ti-O and O-H in TiO2 and TiO2−x nanosheets can be confirmed by their FTIR spectra (Fig. S4). Owing to the presence of O-H, TiO2 and TiO2−x nanosheets both showed negative charge (Fig. S5).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Enhanced photothermal properties of TiO2−x nanosheets
ce:section.ce:para: Having confirmed the formation of oxygen vacancy in TiO2−x nanosheets and their extended NIR-II absorption, we then investigated the photothermal properties of TiO2−x nanosheets in the NIR-I and NIR-II window. TiO2 and TiO2−x nanosheets were initially exposed to 808 or 1064 nm laser at the same concentration (0.3 mg/mL). Fig. 2a revealed that the temperature of TiO2−x solution under 1064 nm laser irradiation (0.6 W/cm2) increased to about 55 oC, which was much higher than that of TiO2 solution (31 °C), indicating the enhanced NIR-II photothermal performance of TiO2−x nanosheets owing to the presence of abundant oxygen vacancies. Then, we investigated the concentration-dependent photothermal behaviors of TiO2−x nanosheets with varied concentration (0–0.3 mg/mL) under the same laser irradiation (1064 nm, 0.6 W/cm2). As presented in Fig. 2b, the elevated temperature of TiO2−x nanosheets under the treatment of NIR-II laser increased as the concentration of TiO2−x nanosheets increasing. Besides, the laser power density-dependent photothermal behaviors was also observed in TiO2−x nanosheets (0.3 mg/mL) by increasing the power density of 1064 nm laser from 0.4 to 0.8 W/cm2 (Fig. 2c). The dramatically increased temperature of TiO2−x nanosheets can be detected in their infrared thermal images (Fig. S6) during the 5 min irradiation period, illustrating that TiO2−x nanosheets can efficiently convert the absorbed NIR laser energy into heat. Moreover, the excellent photothermal conversion behaviors of TiO2−x nanosheets in the NIR-I window can be observed in Fig. S7 and S8.

We then measured and calculated the NIR-II PCE of TiO2−x nanosheets to evaluate their photothermal conversion properties. According to the maximum steady-state temperature and the time constant for heat transfer (Fig. 2d, e), the PCE of TiO2−x nanosheets was calculated to be 69.5 % and 66.7 % in the NIR-II and NIR-I window, respectively, which was much higher than that of widely reported PTT agents, such as noble metal nanoparticles, organic small molecules, and carbon-based nanomaterials. The photothermal stability of TiO2−x nanosheets under 1064 nm laser irradiation was investigated by ten heating and cooling circle, which exhibited no significant temperature deterioration during the recycling (Fig. 2f), highlighting the potential of TiO2−x nanosheets as a durable photothermal agent for NIR-II PTT.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Deep-tissue photothermal properties of TiO2−x nanosheets
ce:section.ce:para: To emulate the clinical scenario, we explored the deep tissue photothermal conversion capabilities of TiO2−x nanosheets in the NIR-I and NIR-II laser. TiO2−x solution was covered by chicken breast tissue of various thicknesses (0–10 mm) and then irradiated by 1064 or 808 nm laser at the power density of 0.8 or 0.4 W/cm2 for 5 min ( Fig. 3a). Compared with the NIR-I laser, the higher temperature variation of TiO2−x nanosheets was observed under the NIR-II laser irradiation in the presence of additional tissue at the same thickness (Fig. 3b), suggesting the deeper penetrability of 1064 nm laser. Specifically, a temperature increase of about 25 oC can be obtained in TiO2−x solution at the 10 mm thickness of additional tissue. Such a temperature increment was well above the required temperature rise and sufficient to ablate cancer cells in living mice.

Encouraged by the excellent deep-tissue photothermal properties of TiO2−x nanosheets in the NIR-II window, we then evaluated their photothermal therapy capability in the cellular level using human cervical cancer Hela cells as the model. The biocompatibility of TiO2−x nanosheets was initially assessed using LO2 and Hela cells. As shown in Fig. 4a and S9a, no significant decrease of cell viability of Hela cells after the treatment of TiO2−x nanosheets can be observed, indicating their good biocompatibility. We also investigated the cell viability of LO2 cells under 1064 nm laser irradiation with varied power density (0–0.8 W/cm2). As depicted in Fig. S9b, no significant cytotoxicity of LO2 cells under NIR-II laser irradiation can be detected, indicating the excellent safety of 1064 nm laser even at the power density of 0.8 W/cm2. In the presence of 1064 nm laser, the cell viability of Hela cells sharply decreased as the concentration of TiO2−x nanosheets increasing (Fig. 4b), suggesting the excellent NIR-II PTT of TiO2−x nanosheets in vitro. Apart from MTT assay, we also utilized live/dead cell staining of Hela cells treated with TiO2−x nanosheets plus 1064 nm laser irradiation to evaluate the excellent therapeutic effect of NIR-II PTT. Fig. 4c revealed that almost all of Hela cells were dead after incubation of TiO2−x nanosheets under 1064 nm laser irradiation, confirming the efficient NIR-II PTT.

The passive targeted ability of TiO2−x nanosheets was then evaluated through NIR imaging. The FDA-approved NIR fluorescence dye ICG was utilized to label TiO2−x nanosheets. After intravenous injection of ICG-labeled TiO2−x nanosheets, Hela tumor bearing mice were imaged using the IVIS Lumina III in vivo Imaging System at the different time points. As depicted in Fig. 5b, TiO2−x nanosheets were accumulated in the tumor tissue after intravenous injection, demonstrating the excellent tumor passive targeted ability of TiO2−x nanosheets. Moreover, the highest NIR fluorescence intensity of ICG@TiO2−x can be detected at 24 h post-injection (Fig. 5d). In addition, the similar results can be observed in the ex vivo NIR fluorescence imaging (Fig. 5c and S10), indicating that TiO2−x nanosheets mainly accumulated in the tumor tissue. Apart from NIR imaging, we also utilized ICP-MS to evaluate the biodistribution of TiO2−x nanosheets in the major organs and tumor tissue by measuring the content of Ti element. Fig. 4e showed that the content of TiO2−x nanosheets in the tumor tissue was much higher than that of other major organs, clearly suggesting their excellent tumor targeting ability.

The therapeutic effect of NIR-II PTT via TiO2−x nanosheets was then assessed in vivo. Hela tumor-bearing mice were randomly divided into four groups including control, 1064, TiO2−x, and TiO2−x + 1064. After intravenous injection of TiO2−x nanosheets (3 mg/kg) for 24 h, 1064 nm laser irradiation was performed for 5 min. The temperature variation of tumor tissue in mice during NIR-II laser irradiation was monitored by an IR thermal camera (Fig. S11). Fig. 5f revealed that the tumor volume in control, 1064, and TiO2−x groups rapidly increased within 14 days, while that in TiO2−x + 1064 group were successfully eliminated, demonstrating the excellent therapeutic effect of NIR-II PTT. Moreover, the survival time of mice after NIR-II PTT via TiO2−x nanosheets was significantly prolonged compared with other groups (Fig. 5g), suggesting the tumor was successfully eliminated without recurrence. In addition, the number of nodules metastasized to the lung was completely eliminated by NIR-II PTT via TiO2−x nanosheets (Fig. S12). H&E and TUNEL staining of tumors was also performed after different treatments for 24 h, revealing that the Hela tumors in TiO2−x + 1064 group were completely damaged (Fig. 5h and S13). We also performed the Ki67 staining to investigate the therapeutic effect of NIR-II PTT via TiO2−x nanosheets. Ki67 staining demonstrated that the control group, 1064 nm laser irradiation alone group, and TiO2−x alone group retained a normal tumor morphology with distinguishable membranes and nuclear structures (Fig. S14). Significantly, the obvious apoptosis and necrosis features were observed in the TiO2−x + 1064 group, confirming the efficient therapeutic effect of NIR-II PTT.

Finally, the potential adverse effect of NIR-II PTT via TiO2−x nanosheets was evaluated. The mice weight in each groups exhibited no obvious decrease during the treatment periods (Fig. S15), indicating the insignificant long-term toxicity of TiO2−x nanosheets. In addition, after the treatment of NIR-II PTT, the major organs were collected for histopathological measurements. No significant histological changes of major organs can be observed in each group (Fig. S16). Moreover, various biochemical blood markers and normal hematology parameters in each group exhibited negligible changes compared with the control group (Fig. S17), suggesting the excellent biosafety of TiO2−x nanosheets.

In summary, we successfully synthesized abundant oxygen-vacancy-doped TiO2−x nanosheets through the hydrothermal method and further solid phase reduction method. The obtained TiO2−x nanosheets exhibited enhanced absorption and photothermal conversion properties in the efficient NIR-II window compared with those of the pristine TiO2 nanosheets. The PCE of TiO2−x nanosheets under 808 or 1064 nm laser irradiation at the safe power density (0.3 W/cm2 for 808 nm and 0.6 W/cm2 for 1064 nm) was determined to be 66.7 % and 69.5 %, respectively. Through the oxygen vacancy enhanced PTT in the NIR-II window via TiO2−x nanosheets, complete tumor recession without recurrence was achieved. Our findings demonstrated that the oxygen-vacancy-doped TiO2−x nanosheets possessed great potential as a NIR-II PTT agent for efficiently treating cervical cancer.

The white TiO2 nanosheets were synthesized in the drying oven through hydrothermal method by using tetrabutyl titanate (50 mL) as the precursors. After adding HF (6 mL) to the tetrabutyl titanate solution, the hydrothermal reaction was carried out at 180 oC for 24 h. The obtained white TiO2 nanosheet sample was then washed with DI water and ethanol through centrifugation.

The black TiO2−x nanosheets with abundant oxygen vacancies were synthesized from white TiO2 nanosheets by using NaBH4 as the reductant. Briefly, the TiO2 nanosheets and NaBH4 at the same weight were mixed in a tube and transferred to tube furnace. After heating at 400 °C for 1 h in the N2 atmosphere, the obtained TiO2−x nanosheets were washed with HCl solution (1 M) for 3 h to remove unreacted NaBH4. Then, the black TiO2−x nanosheets were washed with DI water and ethanol through centrifugation.

TEM images of TiO2−x nanosheets were obtained by a JEM-2100 F electron microscope. The structural characterizations including XPS, XRD, FTIR of TiO2 and TiO2−x nanosheets were performed using Kratos Axis Ultra DLD spectrometer, Rigaku 18 KW D/max-2550, Nicolet AVATAR 370, respectively. A Malvern Zetasizer Nano ZS90 analyzer was utilized to measure the hydrodynamic diameter and Zeta potential of TiO2 and TiO2−x nanosheets. The absorption spectrum ranging from UV to NIR-II region of TiO2−x nanosheets was carried out using Agilent Cary 5000 spectrophotometers.

TiO2−x nanosheet solution with varied concentrations (0–0.3 mg/mL) were exposed to 808 or 1064 nm laser at the varied power density to investigate the concentration-dependent and power-density-dependent photothermal conversion behaviors. The infrared thermal imaging camera was utilized to determine the temperature of TiO2−x nanosheets. For the photothermal stability measurements, TiO2−x nanosheet solution was irradiated by 808 or 1064 nm laser for ten on/off cycles. The measurement of PCE was then performed by recording the temperature change of TiO2−x nanosheets under 808 or 1064 nm laser irradiation. The PCE was calculated according to the previous reports.[13].

To evaluate the deep-tissue photothermal conversion properties of TiO2−x nanosheets, the chicken breast muscles with varied thickness (0–10 mm) were covered in the TiO2−x solution. Then, the 808 or 1064 nm laser was carried out to irradiation the TiO2−x solution. The infrared thermal imaging camera was utilized to determine the temperature of TiO2−x nanosheets.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro biocompatibility and NIR-II PTT
ce:section.ce:para: Human cervical cancer Hela cells were purchased from Cell Bank of Type Culture Collection of Chinese Academy of Sciences (Shanghai, China) and cultured in DMEM medium in the presence of 10 % fetal bovine serum. For the evaluation of biocompatibility, the relatively viability of Hela cells after treating with TiO2−x nanosheets was initially detected by a standard MTT assay according to previous reports [32]. For the NIR-II PTT, TiO2−x nanosheets were added to the Hela cells and incubated for 4 h. Then, Hela cells were irradiated by 808 or 1064 nm laser for 5 min. After irradiation, Hela cells were incubated for another 24 or 48 h. Finally, a standard MTT assay was carried out to determine the NIR-II PTT efficiency of TiO2−x nanosheets.

For live/dead staining, calcein AM and PI were utilized to stain the Hela cells under different treatments (including control, 1064, TiO2−x, and TiO2−x + 1064). In group 4, Hela cells were incubated with TiO2−x for 4 h, followed by 1064 irradiation (0.6 W/cm2) for 5 min. The fluorescence microscope (Olympus, Japan) was performed to obtain the images.

To evaluate the tumor targeting ability of TiO2−x nanosheets, the FDA-approved NIR fluorescence dye ICG was utilized to label TiO2−x nanosheets. Mice bearing Hela tumors were administrated by intravenous injection with ICG@TiO2−x (3 mg/kg). At different time points, the NIR fluorescence images were captured using a IVIS Lumina III in vivo Imaging System (PerkinElmer, USA).

Balb/c nude mice with the age of five weeks were purchased from the SLAC Laboratory Animal Co. Ltd. (Shanghai, China). 1 × 106 Hela cells were subcutaneously injected into the axillary of nude mice to establish the tumor models. After tumor volume increased to about 100 mm3, mice were randomly divided into four groups as followed: control, 1064 (0.6 W/cm2), TiO2−x (3 mg/kg), and TiO2−x (3 mg/kg) + 1064 (0.6 W/cm2). The NIR-II laser irradiation was carried out after the intravenous injection of TiO2−x nanosheets for 24 h. The temperature changes of tumor sites were monitored by a Fluke infrared thermal imaging camera. After treatments, tumor size was measured every other day and mice body weight was measured every day. The major organs and tumor were collected for histology measurements. In addition, blood was collected from the orbital of treated mice for serum biochemistry assay and complete blood panel analysis.

All data were represented as mean ± standard error of triplicate samples from three independent experiments. *denotes a statistical significance (*p < 0.05, **p < 0.01 and ***p < 0.001) between the experimental data of two groups.

Lihua Bian: Investigation, Methodology, Writing - original draft. Nan Wang: Investigation, Methodology. Kelibiguli Tuersong: Visualization, Methodology, Formal analysis. Ayiguzaili Kaidierdan: Visualization, Formal analysis. Jinfeng Li: Investigation, Methodology. Jing Gong: Conceptualization, Supervision, Writing - review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.colsurfb.2023.113427.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
Supplementary material
.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Enriched graphitic N dopants of carbon dots as F cores mediate photothermal conversion in the NIR-II window with high efficiency

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: B. Geng, W. Shen, F. Fang, H. Qin, P. Li, X. Wang, X. Li, D. Pan, L. Shen, Enriched graphitic N dopants of carbon dots as F cores mediate photothermal conversion in the NIR-II window with high efficiency, Carbon 162 (2020) 220–233.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: NIR-responsive carbon dots for efficient photothermal cancer therapy at low power densities

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: B. Geng, D. Yang, D. Pan, L. Wang, F. Zheng, W. Shen, C. Zhang, X. Li, NIR-responsive carbon dots for efficient photothermal cancer therapy at low power densities, Carbon 134 (2018) 153–162.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Nanocatalysts-augmented and photothermal-enhanced tumor-specific sequential nanocatalytic therapy in both NIR-I and NIR-II biowindows
